I discuss it in some detail in my book Circles of Power, from a Golden Dawn perspective. Eliphas Levi also devotes a chapter to it in Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic.

As for the werewolf traditions in the Pacific Northwest, I read about them in the very good collection of Northwest anthropological literature in the University of Washington's collections in Seattle, then asked a friend with extensive contacts "on the res" about it. comments.title: A small spell cast
comments.content:
It is probably ok to break silence on this one. Ireland went to the polls last Friday. During my SOP that morning I visualised my "cross of light" connecting me to the whole of the land, and I visualised all the blessings as flowing out through that connection across the land. I then set up a candle to burn from the time the polls opened at 7am until they closed at 10pm, positioned over my written intention, which I also held in mind as often as I thought of it throughout the day.

Intention was written thusly: "Fear ebbs, love flows in, votes cast with clear eye, yes wins"

"Yes" did win (the politics are very well detailed and considered elsewhere online) by a hugely significant margin, at 66.3% to 33.4% of votes cast.

I do think my spell was enhanced by the practical work of canvassing and persuading work I had been caught up in for the 6 weeks previously.

I also do believe I could live with any blowback that might come from this intention, as expressed - which I had meditated about for some time.

I guess I would like to know if you have any view on how this kind of political, magical intention stacks up against others you've described.

replies.title: Re: A small spell cast
replies.content:
Precise, economical, and effective. I note that you had a positive intention very precisely aimed at your goal; you grounded your working by also engaging in practical action on the material plane; and you worked through the consequences in meditation.

Thus, you achieved your intention. Nicely done.

comments.title: Masturbation and magic
comments.content: Dear JMG, You've written on this blog about the polarity created by sexual intercourse but I was wondering about the effect of masturbation on magical practice. In my reading it is often something some authors get in bit of a state about but is this out dated morality or something deeper?

replies.title: Re: Masturbation and magic
replies.content:
As far as I can tell, the only reason it's an issue is that Victorians got hysterical about the subject. (There was actually an organization called the White Cross in Britain and America dedicated to stamping out masturbation.) Sexual behavior generally doesn't have any particular impact on magic unless you specifically set out to include it -- whether than means via ritual intercourse, the kind of polarity work that doesn't involve actual contact, or celibacy and the redirection of sexual energies into other channels -- all of which are options, but none of which are required.

Remember that many of the authors of older occult books had to deal with the weird bit of Christian rhetoric that assumes that occultism is all about having lots of casual sex. Thus there was often a bit of moral posturing in their books, especially on a subject such as masturbation, which would make them look properly moral in the eyes of the mainstream, and would also chase off those people who were looking for lots of casual sex and thought they could get it via occultism!

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Good day Mr Greer,

I’ve a couple of questions on Geomancy.

Question No. 1 - My parents have a joint bank account and their money was seized by federal prosecutors on account that there were cash deposits made by a firm that’s under investigation for tax evasion. A potential lawsuit could drag Pa, Ma.

I cast a geomancy chart and the question was:
“Will my parents’ money be unblocked?”
The mothers , 1st thru 4th were, respectively, Fortuna Major, Via, Via and Laetitia.

I checked the 2nd house (Via) but then I reasoned it’s my parents’ money, not mine.
So I checked the 5th house (Caput Draconis) as it stands for my father’s money.
But wait... What about my mother’s money ?
There we go, on to the 11th house (Tristitia) for it speaks of my mother’s money.
Would it be” other people’s money” in 8th house (Cauda Draconis)?

How would you read the chart?
Also, could I look for information on the potential lawsuit using the same chart? Is that acceptable? Or should I cast a new chart?
Either way, where would “lawsuits” be positioned?
On the 7th house?
On the 10th house (7th house from my father’s perspective)?
On the 4th house (7th house from my mother’s perspective)?
On the 2nd house(7th house from other people’s Money)?

The road is long with many a winding turn...


Question No. 2 - I know there are several ways of laying the figures in the House Chart. So I guess one has to acknowledge that there will be different answers for questions like “What Planet Rules my Guardian Genius?”.
I cast the figures once and put them into three diferente layouts.
The first one as given in your book “The Art and Practice of Geomancy”.
The second (Esoteric Method ) and the third (Golden Dawn Method) as given in Stephen Skinner’s Terrestrial Astrology – Divination by Geomancy -Appendix III
The results for the 11th house were:
First layout  the figure VIA (Moon)
Second layout  the figure PUELLA (Venus)
Third layo the figure RUBEUS (Mars)
Interestingly enough I was born under Aries (Mars). My rising sign is Cancer (Moon).
I was also born under Aries Moon so the sign the Moon entered next was Taurus (Venus).

Is it a far-fetched idea that we could have more than one Guardian Genius like a shaman that has many helping spirits?
Iamblichus’ “Theurgia or On the Mysteries of Egypt” says “...One Guardian Demon Only to an Individual...”

What do you say?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
Beginners routinely land themselves in confusions of this sort. That's why I recommend that you start out with shield charts, where you can take the judge and witnesses as the answer, and move to house charts only when you've gotten a good enough sense of the system that you can deal with a complex pattern.

I'd cast one reading about the money and another about the potential for a lawsuit. I'd choose one of my parents to ask about, even though it's a joint account, in order to avoid unnecessary confusions. It would be best to use your father, since that way he's the fourth, his money is the fifth, the government is the tenth (this stays the same, since it's your government too), and the threat of legal penalties is the third (which is his twelfth); from the figures in these houses, and the relationships between them, I'd then synthesize the answer.

As far as your second question, choose one system and cast a divination with that in mind. Doing what you've done is like writing someone a letter in English and then trying to read the answer as though it's French.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:

Actually I did cast the divination for the Guardian Genius name using only the system you prescribe in “The Art and Practice of Geomancy”.
It was only after I had read Skinner's book that I got curious of what the results would be. And since I already had a shield chart for that question, you know, I thought I'd give wings to my imagination.
Besides I tend to think that there are many truths under different disguises. That is why I asked you if you think we can have more than one Genius even though Iamblichus would say no.

Now let me put another question if I may:

You say in your book that when casting for 'the name of the Genius'we can use the cardines as Mothers to draw up a new chart that outlines the gifts and powers in detail, right? Well, which cardine is 1st Mother, 2nd Mother, and so forth?
Thank you

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: Tenth house becomes the first mother, first house becomes the second, fourth house becomes the third, and seventh house becomes the fourth. Take it from there and interpret in the usual way.

replies.title: Re: deities from different traditions
replies.content: Your relationship to a deity is as irreducibly personal as a love affair, and no one else can tell you what is or isn't right for you. All I can suggest is that you do your homework, find out as much as possible about the deities in question, and pay attention to whether the tradition that worships that deity is or isn't comfortable with having people from other cultures join in that worship. (Buddhists tend to be very enthusiastic about other people invoking Buddhist deities such as Kwan Yin; I'll defer to the Hindus among us as to whether they have the same attitude.)

replies.title: Re: deities from different traditions

Lots of people work with deities from different traditions and cultural backgrounds. Sometimes that's even hardwired into a system -- in the Golden Dawn, for example, the Christian trinity and the ancient Egyptian gods were both worked, often in the same ritual.

As for deities and cultural borrowing, opinions vary and so may the attitudes of different deities, and that's why I recommend paying attention to what their worshipers say.

replies.title: Re: cleansing...
replies.content: There are several good ways to do this sort of thing. One of the best -- so long as the house isn't carpeted! -- is to do a floor wash. Suggest that she get a bottle of Florida Water -- this is a cologne much used in traditional conjure -- some kosher salt, and the hot spice of her choice (white pepper is the one I usually use). To a bucket of mop water, add half a cup of Florida water, a tablespoon of salt, and a teaspoon of the hot spice. Then take a new mop that's never been used for any other purpose, and mop the entire house with it, beginning in the kitchen (the symbolic heart of the house) and ending at the front door. Once this is done, burn a blessing and purifying incense such as frankincense in the kitchen and, if they shared a bed, in the bedroom. That'll clean the place energetically and bring in positive energies for the future.

replies.title: Re: New planets, alien magics
replies.content:
This kind of thing has been happening in astrology since the discovery of Uranus back in 1784, so astrologers are used to it. They'd put the newly discovered planet into charts, and start tracking the way its influence seems to affect them and their clients when activated by transits, progressions, primary arcs, and so forth. It takes a few years, but within a decade or so interpretations converge on a common set of themes.

The discovery of a planet does seem to have a significant effect on human society, as new social factors come into play beginning about 30 years (a Saturn cycle) before a planet's official discovery. When a celestial body loses its status as a planet -- this happened to Ceres around 1850 and to Pluto in 2006 -- there's a thirty-year window as the influence fades out.

replies.title: Re: Electricity
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender)

Electrical charge is one of the fundamental organizing principles of matter down to the subatomic level. FWIW, it would logically follow that if the cosmos, or at least the material part of the cosmos, is neutral to living things, electricity is probably neutral. If the organizing principle of the cosmos is beneficent, then electricity may be beneficent, or at least more beneficent than some other physical force that an Original Source might employ to organize matter.

Moving down a couple of levels, electrical charges and currents might have some kind of ruling intelligence that could be addressed and worked with. If one's job involves dealing with electrical charges, potentials and currents, one might experiment with this.

I do think that complex electrical circuits can be treated as if they were sentient, the way some people interact with an automobile that has good days and bad days.

comments.title: Mental Magic
comments.content:
Archdruid,

We've been slowly working our way through Atkinson's book in our new book group, and last weeks one of your readers asked you a question about mental magic that got me wondering. As someone who has aspergers, have you ever used mental magic to try and improve your ability to pick up non-verbal cues? Even if just temporarily? Is such a thing even possible?

Regards,

replies.title: Re: Mental Magic
replies.content: Good question! It's not something I've tried -- my approach was always to aim for end states such as functioning well in groups of people, and leave the mechanics up to the magic. As to whether it's possible, I have no idea -- sounds like something worth experimenting with!

replies.title: Re: Mental Magic
replies.content:
From my own experience, I can answer the latter question: it is absolutely possible. However, the "even if just temporarily" part suggests that how you're imagining such an alteration might work differs significantly from how I experience it working. Substituting "even if just when I make the deliberate effort to do so" would put you much closer.

By analogy, one doesn't practice the Art of Memory by affirming over and over again, "I have a perfect memory!" until it comes true (even if just temporarily!). It works by starting out with very nearly the opposite: recognizing that your "raw" memory is just terrible at certain things (like memorizing lists of arbitrary unrelated words), and learning to adapt things it's much better at (like recalling narratives and familiar arrangements of objects in space) to those purposes instead.

I've never had a formal diagnosis but I have so much in common with so many of the aspergers people I've met that I assume I'm on that spectrum somewhere. There are tests online that ask, "what emotion is this [photograph of a] person showing?" and it might as well be asking me to read Chinese newspaper headlines. Such exercises generally confirm two things:

1. I don't automatically perceive many nonverbal cues.

2. Most people do perceive many nonverbal cues, without any noticeable effort on their part, and hence they expect me to respond to theirs.

There are other narratives I could derive instead or in addition, but they aren't as accurate or useful. "I'm oblivious and inattentive" is one. "The problem must be I don't really care what other people feel" is another. "This is so unfair" is yet another. One of the most insidiously counterproductive is "I have to try harder." (That works just about as well as trying harder to read Chinese newspaper headlines when I don't know any Chinese.)

The first acknowledgement already suggests one effective mental strategy: I don't perceive nonverbal cues automatically, but what about perceiving them deliberately? Looking for them and thinking about them, in other words, perhaps with the aid of learning from books on the subject, which do exist. If you don't know Chinese it does no good to try harder to read a newspaper headline in Chinese, but one can learn Chinese. I've read that some of the top experts in nonverbal communication are folks with asperger's, because instead of relying on a vague general innate perception of it as most people do, they learned it deliberately and consciously and in thorough detail instead. Along similar lines, my own nephew, who shares some of my traits, studied acting. (He just graduated a few days ago from a four-year Theater Arts program.)

The second acknowledgement leads (a bit more indirectly) to a second mental strategy: I don't have to read others' nonverbal cues to respond to them, if I already know what those cues are saying. (To extend the previous analogy: if you know what the current news is, you know what the Chinese newspaper headlines say, even if you can't read them.) The game is, be the other person. Role-play them in your imagination. Give their thoughts and feelings space in your mind. In other words, read their minds. It helps if you know them well, but even if you don't, you can make a very good start by imagining what you would be thinking and feeling, if you were standing where they are, if what's being said to them were being said to you, and so forth. This puts to use the most important principle I know: other people aren't nearly as different from you as you think.

The second approach seems more magical than the first, but that might not really be the case. (As my question last week demonstrates, I'm still feeling this out myself).

replies.content:

replies.content: Start with two old classics, The Old Straight Track by Alfred Watkins and The View Over Atlantis by John Michell. replies.title: Re: Resurrection of Jesus
replies.content:
The insistence on the physical resurrection of Jesus is one of the things about Christianity I've always found just that little bit too much to take. It's always seemed to me -- as in, from childhood on -- that Christians have gotten the reality of myths confused with the reality of matter; it's as though they're trying to insist that this once, somebody really did draw an absolutely perfect circle. Sallust's neat definition -- "myths are things that never happened but always are" -- makes more sense to me.

My guess -- and since I wasn't there, you know, it's necessarily a guess! -- is that belief in the resurrection of Jesus got going among the earliest Christians as a response to cognitive dissonance. They'd convinced themselves that this guy from Galilee really was the Messiah, the Son of David prophesied in the Old Testament, and then the Romans crucified him, end of story. Just as the UFO believers chronicled in that classic sociological study When Prophecy Fails came up with elaborate theories to explain away the fact that their beliefs had been disproved by events, my guess is that some of the followers of Jesus convinced themselves that he must have been resurrected, and was sitting in Heaven waiting to come back to earth any minute now, as a way to avoid having to face up to the cataclysmic failure of beliefs on which they'd staked everything they had. Then Saul of Tarsus converted to this belief, redefined it in terms familiar to all the dying-and-rising gods of the Hellenistic east, and took it on the road with him...and Christianity as we know it was born.

That doesn't mean Christianity is wrong, by the way. Again, myths are things that never happened but always are; thus the resurrection, like the other events in the life of Jesus, are enduring spiritual realities, not historical events. Yes, I know a great many Christians won't agree with this!

replies.title: Re: Regarding Enochian magic
replies.content: It's not a matter of specificity of purpose, it's a matter of specificity of forces. When you summon a spirit from one of the Enochian tablets, or invoke one of the specific divine names from the tablets, you're getting a very precisely defined energy, much less balanced and equilibrated than the energies of the elemental, planetary, or Sephirothic forces worked by the rest of the Golden Dawn system. If I may, this is usually called the Doctrine of Signatures. The explanation offered by Matthew Wood, who I believe took his ideas from Rudolf Steiner is that a plant adapts itself to a specific environmental niche. To do so it may begin to have a form similar that resembles a body part. Thus where it grows, what it looks like may inform its traditional use.

An example would be Plantain (Plantago major) it's environment is compacted earth, and so it is good at pulling up water and nutrients. Likewise it is traditionally considered to be good at pulling out insect venom or dirt from a wound (something I have observed personally). Likewise it has a shape resembling a tongue, and so it is historically appropriated to the mouth. Likewise, it is traditionally considered specific for issues in the mouth, especially, according to Matthew Wood and Galen, a swollen tongue. I believe that chewing on Plantain leaves saved me from an expensive trip to a dentist when I had severe swelling, pain and pus in an incoming wisdom tooth while I was 19 years-old and wwoofing in Appalachia.

Interestingly, Rudolf Steiner noticed that an animal embryo is curled in on itself whereas plants grow outward. There outward form is also "who they are" in a way that is very different than animals. Plants directly receive the sun whereas animals eat and thus receive energy. Plants then don't exist representationally. They interface directly with their energy source whereas human have a "solar plexus" by which to organize digestion. Plants then outwardly exist "as above so below," whereas with animals this maxim is expressed significantly more subtly.

comments.title: Evolution of Consciousness
comments.content:
What´s your take on the Steiner-Barfield idea of “evolution of consciousness”, more specifically the idea that ancient peoples (say the ancient Greeks of the heroic period) had a fundamentally different consciousness from our own, including primitive clairvoyance, no I-consciousness, etc. I sometimes wonder whether this could be true, since some human cultures really do seem different from each other on a deep level (thus, the Indus Valley Civilization was peaceful and probably egalitarian, while most high cultures are war-prone, etc). What accounts for these changes, if they are real – impersonal forces, or “time-angels” as in Steiner? (I assume an evolutionary biologist would argue that we´re dealing with some kind of mutations!)

Tidlösa

replies.title: Re: Evolution of Consciousness
replies.content:
I see the Steiner-Barfield theory as a dumbed-down, oversimplified version of Giambattista Vico's far more interesting cyclic theory of consciousness. As Vico points out, the ancient Greeks of the heroic period had pretty much the same state of consciousness as the Europeans of the Dark Ages,and in both cases things moved through the same track from there to the complex, abstract, and brittle consciousness we and the late Romans share. Though Vico lived too early to know this, the same cyclic process has happened in every other civilization of which we have record, so it's part of the ordinary cycle of rise and fall.

I'm far from sure we know enough about the Indus Valley civilization to know that it was peaceful and egalitarian -- do you recall the days when these same claims were made about the Mayans? I certainly do, and the Mayans turned out to be nothing of the kind. (Many other ancient societies have had this set of fantasies projected onto them, for that matter.) It's nonetheless true that, as Spengler points out, every high culture has its own unique take on the world. One can conceptualize the guiding force behind that unique take as a spirit or angel, or as an impersonal force, depending on your philosophical preferences; "every energy is an entity, and every entity is an energy," says a useful magical maxim.

replies.title: Re: meditation posture
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender)

You may be surrounded by the tree's subtle body, especially if you are sitting very close to the trunk. The trunk contains a large part of the tree's circulatory system, which is active whenever the tree is not dormant.

Many years ago, I took a class on Western geomancy from Richard Feather Anderson. It was nothing to do with the system of geomantic divination discussed here; it was about sacred geometry, earth energies, western counterparts of feng shui, and the like. The main thing I took away from the class was an exercise we did of meditating with a tree.

One of the elementary methods of grounding and centering that is widely taught in neopagan circles is called the Tree of Life meditation (nothing to do with the Cabbalistic Tree of Life). It is often done standing but may be done sitting. It starts by imagining that roots are growing from the soles of your feet deep into the earth, and drawing up energy from the earth's core. I'm not going to write the rest of the directions today, but the basic idea is to imaginatively shape one's energy system to be connected with the sun, the atmosphere, and the earth as if one were a leafy tree. You can probably find the directions in a book.

In the class, Richard pointed out that one way to find out the history and current condition of a local patch of the landscape would be to inquire of a mature, healthy tree, since the tree has lived in that spot for a long time. He took us on a field trip to a quiet place that had a lot of long-lived trees (the grounds of the Palace of Fine Arts near the northeastern corner of San Francisco). We were instructed to spread out, find a suitable tree, and ask it if it was willing to have one join it for a meditation. If the tree was willing, the student was to sit with his back to the tree, get calm, and do the Tree of Life meditation, but align his own energy system with the tree's, so that they would be in resonance or merge to an extent. After doing the meditation that way for awhile, one would have some access to the tree's awareness. One could then ask the tree questions about its memory of the place, or about current conditions.

It worked for me, although the mental interaction with the tree was brief, because it was the second tree I picked and I did not have time to slow my own thought processes down enough to have a deep conversation with the tree.

replies.title: Re: meditation posture
replies.content: Thank you for the description of the tree meditation!  This sounds like something I should do with my mature trees here on the property.

replies.title: Re: meditation posture
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender)

Minor revisions: I meant the northwestern corner of SF. We did the meditation sitting up with our back resting against the tree trunk. The tree should be healthy and fully grown, and you should inspect the trunk for ants before resting against it!

I don't know whether JMG approves of the kind of energy meditation I'm about to describe, but for those who are curious, here are general directions for the basic tree meditation as it's usually done. Stand comfortably, relax, close your eyes, take a few deep breaths. Imagine/visualize roots growing from the soles of your feet into the earth. Let the roots grow deeper, until they reach the core (I don't do this part). Draw up earth energy and circulate throughout your body. Open the chakra at the top of your skull, allow cosmic energy (or solar energy, if you prefer) to flow in. Visualize the energy circulating through your entire body, mixing with the earth energy, and both spilling out of hands, feet and top of head. If doing this while standing in a circle with other people, everyone would be directed to intertwine roots with the others, and imagine arms as branches with energy intertwining with the branches of others. Then open the eyes and get on with whatever you intend to do.

The visualization does not have to be exact and detailed. The object is to increase one's body-mind connection to cosmic and telluric energies and gently to clear out blockages. Doing this or something similar such as (some of) the meditations JMG recommends is a good practice before doing magic, because it helps to restore awareness that one is an energy being, connected not isolated from the cosmos. It also primes the pump so that one is not limited to the energy already present in one's body.
